text: Today I am presenting a treatment of superior mesenteric artery syndrome after laparoscopic gastric bypass. Our patient was a thirty-three year old female who underwent laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass in two thousand six. She presented to us after having multiple episodes of epigastric pain radiating to her back, exacerbated with oral intake and associated with nausea and vomiting. Initial workup showed lipase greater than one thousand consistent with pancreatitis. EGD, MRI, and CT were notable only for dilated remnant stomach and duodenum in absence of choledochalothiasis or other biliary anomalies. Here are representative images of the CT scan. Note the dilated remnant stomach and duodenum.

Axial CT images show the obstruction at the point where the superior mesenteric artery crosses the third portion of the duodenum. During double balloon endoscopy, the first and second portions of the duodenum appear clearly dilated, with resistance observed as the endoscope passes beyond the SMA, resembling a valve phenomenon. No further dilation is visible beyond this point.

The surgeon enters the left midabdomen laparoscopically after insufflation and places three additional ports. Dissection begins by identifying the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis and tracing the biliary limb back towards the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon lyses multiple filmy adhesions to mobilize the biliary limb and transverse colon. The transverse colon is fully mobilized, revealing the dilated duodenum through the mesocolon in the lower right screen. A space is created through the mesocolon, positioning the duodenum for anastomosis.

The surgeon aligns the biliary limb of the jejunum along the duodenum using silk sutures. Enterotomies are created and dilated with cautery. A 60mm tri-stapler is used to perform the anastomosis, and the enterotomy is closed longitudinally with a running 3-0 silk suture.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and closes the mesocolic defect with a running silk suture from the mesocolon to the jejunal limb. Post-operative progress is noted with the patient advancing to clear liquids on day one, experiencing initial bloating that improves, and by day three, tolerating a regular post-gastric bypass diet before discharge.
